content.headline: The browned bits stuck to your pan after searing are pure concentrated flavor — most home cooks wash them down the drain.
points: Deglaze while the pan is still hot — liquid added to a cold pan won't lift the fond
Scrape the bottom firmly as soon as liquid goes in — every brown bit is flavor, and they dissolve in seconds while the pan is hot
Reduce the liquid by half to concentrate flavor before mounting butter — thin sauce is under-reduced sauce
Mount with cold butter off the heat, swirling constantly — hot butter breaks the emulsion and pools as grease on top
content.tell_me_more: The explosive sizzle and steam cloud when liquid hits the hot pan is called deglazing, and it works because the temperature differential between the hot fond and the cold liquid causes rapid dissolving. This is why you scrape immediately — the fond is most soluble in that first 30 seconds. Wait too long and the fond re-adheres to the pan as the temperature equalizes. The whole technique is a race: liquid in, scrape fast, get every bit before the pan cools.

missions.title: Chicken Pan Sauce
missions.description: Sear one chicken thigh in a stainless steel or cast iron pan, remove it to rest, then build a pan sauce using half a cup of chicken stock and one tablespoon of cold butter.
missions.why_it_matters: Stainless steel or cast iron shows the fond clearly — nonstick pans don't develop it the same way.
missions.prompt: Show the finished pan sauce in the pan or spooned over the rested chicken. The sauce should look glossy and slightly thick — not thin and watery like plain stock, not gloppy like gravy. It should coat the back of a spoon and have a warm brown color from the fond.
reflection_choices: Sauce is glossy, coats a spoon, and tastes rich and complex — got all the fond up
Sauce tastes good but is thinner than expected — needed more reduction or more butter
Good consistency but some fond stayed stuck on the pan — didn't scrape aggressively enough
Sauce broke — pools of fat floating in thin liquid — butter went in when pan was too hot

missions.title: The Fond Moment
missions.description: Reflect on what happened when the liquid hit the pan and how quickly you scraped.
missions.why_it_matters: The deglazing moment is the whole technique — understanding it in detail means you'll do it correctly without thinking.
missions.prompt: What happened when the liquid went into the hot pan — describe the sound, the steam, and how fast the liquid changed color? Did you get all the fond up, or did some stick to the pan? And when you tasted the finished sauce, could you taste the difference the fond made compared to what plain stock would taste like?
